text:
A Guardian Spirit Beast (, Shugo Reijū) is a parasitic-type Nen beast created by the Seed Urn Ceremony. Participants of the ceremony each become a host to one Guardian Spirit Beast. All participants/hosts seen thus far have either been the king or a legitimate prince of the Kakin Empire. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 33, Chapter 349 They are instrumental to the unfolding of the Succession Contest.

History

In the age of rival warlords, Kakin was a small country that was threatened to be absorbed by its neighboring countries. Around that time, according to ancient manuscripts, the first king of Kakin conjured the Seed Urn inspired by "Worm Toxin" and had his children fight in a succession contest for the throne, which is the foundation for Kakin becoming the superpower that it is today. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 362 

The Seed Urn

The Seed Urn, a conjured pot with some facial features, is the pivotal item for the beginning of the Succession War. Those seeking and qualified to be participants partake in the Seed Urn Ceremony to (not necessarily known to them) receive a Guardian Spirit Beast. 

Appearance and Behavior

Guardian Spirit Beasts are parasitic Nen beasts that possess a person related to a deceased, that are born of the deceased's strong desire for the perpetuation of their descendants. They feed on the aura of their hosts as an energy source and their forms and abilities take after their hosts' characters and dispositions. Since Guardian Spirit Beasts are a creation of the Seed Urn and not of a host, hosts cannot control their Guardian Spirit Beasts at will. Thus far, each host has been seen to host exactly one Guardian Spirit Beast. Guardian Spirit Beasts that create other Nen beasts do exist; these "sub-beasts" have not been considered Guardian Spirit Beasts by the story thus far.

The appearances and behaviors of Guardian Spirit Beasts are influenced by their host's character and disposition, and so vary as much as their hosts do — Guardian Spirit Beasts with humanoid, creature-like, spherical, or even formless appearances have been seen. For example:
Tserriednich Hui Guo Rou has strong feelings on two-faced women. His Guardian Spirit Beast is a horse with womanly characteristics, most notably the head of a woman, containing another woman's head inside its mouth. 
Salé-salé Hui Guo Rou seems to care about nothing other than enjoying himself and making others (particularly women) like him. His Guardian Spirit Beast is a mostly featureless spherical mass, covered in mouths that exhale smoke that positively influences the perception that who inhale it have of Salé-salé. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 37, Chapter 381 

Some Guardian Spirit Beasts have been seen responding to changes in the emotional state of their hosts — the Guardian Spirit Beasts of Camilla and Marayam are seen changing their appearances in accordance with their hosts' respective outrage Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 363 and anxiety. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 366 Hunter × Hunter - Volume 36, Chapter 372 

Guardian Spirit Beasts are almost always seen near their hosts. Exceptions exist — Tubeppa's Guardian Spirit Beast generally remains hidden, Hunter × Hunter - Volume 37, Chapter 388 and Momoze's Guardian Spirit Beast has been seen wandering away from her. When Guardian Spirit Beasts are seen near their hosts, they are often seen hovering above them, perched on their shoulders, or simply physically near them, depending on the beast's anatomy. Since they feed on the aura of their hosts as an energy source, Guardian Spirit Beasts vanish when a host does not provide sufficient aura to sustain them, whether due to sheer fatigue Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 366 or because of Zetsu. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 37, Chapter 385 

Guardian Spirit Beasts have varying degrees of sentience; a beast's level of sentience could depend on the general awareness and watchfulness of their hosts. Some Guardian Spirit Beasts have no identifiable head or form; others can identify people that are able to see them, and are varied even in response to these identified people — some stare, Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 365 others intimidate. The levels of sentience that have been seen range from being unaware of lethal threats in close proximity to an awareness of people that are watching it Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 365 to a human-like level of awareness. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 38, Chapter 400 

All Guardian Spirit Beasts created from the same Seed Urn are imbued with two primary instinctual rules:
they do not kill one another; 
they do not directly attack a person possessed by a Guardian Spirit Beast. 

Guardian Spirit Beasts vary greatly in abilities. Some Guardian Spirit Beasts are essentially only "fangs and claws". Others are endowed with abilities that make them skilled in logistical support. Some Guardian Spirit Beasts are even able to half-awaken others to Nen and even endow their hosts with Nen abilities. Guardian Spirit Beasts generally protect their hosts against threats, at least physical (including attempted suicide via bullets) Hunter × Hunter - Volume 37, Chapter 382 and likely against Nen-based threats as well. Just like many other features of Guardian Spirit Beasts, this protection likely depends on the abilities and personality of one's host. Guardian Spirit Beasts are not immune to attacks; they have been eliminated by other Nen abilities. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 37, Chapter 381 The Guardian Spirit Beast of the winner of a succession contest can continue to exist even after their contest has ended, notably, King Nasubi Hui Guo Rou's Guardian Spirit Beast is seen around him. 

Nen-based Properties

Each Guardian Spirit Beast is endowed with its own Nen type, which may differ from its host's. However, its abilities are still influenced by the host's personality and their Nen category. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 36, Chapter 375 A Guardian Spirit Beast cannot manifest itself while the host is in a state of Zetsu, Hunter × Hunter - Volume 36, Chapter 373 and it may find itself unable to protect its host if the latter has run out of aura. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 368 

The host cannot see their own Guardian Spirit Beast, nor can they see those of other hosts from the same generation even if they are a Nen user. Balsamilco surmised that there must be a limitation that prevents the ceremony participants from seeing the Guardian Spirit Beasts or a condition that must be fulfilled before they can do so. A host from a previous generation can see the Guardian Spirit Beasts of the next. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 361 

Kurapika seems to have implied that Guardian Spirit Beasts are conjured; however, unlike many Conjuration abilities, they are invisible not only to the hosts of one but also to all non-users. This makes them a low-risk ability, which should have resulted in a tremendous amount of aura being spent to infuse the 14 princes with one Guardian Spirit Beast each. He, therefore, speculated that a complex system of Vows and Limitations ensures their continued existence and that if a prince managed to pull out of the Succession War, all Guardian Spirit Beasts would disappear. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 36, Chapter 371 Curiously, despite Kurapika's inference, many Guardian Spirit Beasts have shown the ability to phase through solid matter, Hunter × Hunter - Volume 34, Chapter 360 Hunter × Hunter - Volume 35, Chapter 369 a trait which seems to be associated with Emission. Hunter × Hunter - Volume 33, Chapter 347 

Guardian Spirit Beasts

Trivia

"Worm Toxin" (, Kodoku) is a type of Japanese poisonous magic, derivative of the Chinese Gu (, or ) magic. To create it, several poisonous insects are closed together inside a jar, which is opened when only one of them remains. The surviving insect is said to have absorbed the poison of all the other insects.
The inspiration for the Seed Urn is self-evident as the similarities do not stop at the ritual through which the members of the Kakin Empire's Royal Family obtain a Guardian Spirit Beast, but extend to the whole Succession War: the siblings are in fact forced to stay on a ship journeying to the Dark Continent while they attempt to kill each other, with the only survivor becoming the legitimate heir to the throne.

Intertextuality and References

The Seed Urn's facial features highly resembles the facial features of the clay dolls from the late Jōmon period of Japan.
In Togashi's manga YuYu Hakusho, an egg is given to new Spirit Detectives. With time, a Spirit Beast hatches from it, with its appearance, personality, and powers resembling those of the one who keeps it. The whole process is very similar to the Seed Urn Ceremony through which Nasubi and his children obtained their Nen beasts.
When asked to place his hand in the mouth of the Seed Urn, Tserriednich worried it might be bitten off: this may be a reference to "La Bocca della Verità" ("The Mouth of Truth"). Starting from the Middle Ages, it was believed that if one told a lie while his hand was in the mouth of the sculpture, it would be bitten off.
Guardian Spirit Beasts share similarities with the following real-life concepts, myths, and/or beliefs:
Ancient Greek religion and mythology's daemons, which originally referred to a lesser deity or guiding spirit.
Christianity's, Islam's, and Zoroastrianism's guardian angels, an angel that is assigned to protect and guide a particular person, group, kingdom, or country.
Islam's Qarins—unseen spirits, either a Jinn ("devil") or an angel that accompanies a person and influences its host to lead an ungodly or godly life respectively. This can be seen on the royalties of Kakin of unsavory characters that have obscene, repulsive, and sinister-looking beasts.
Tserriednich's Guardian Beast resembles Burāq (from Islam), a horse-like creature with a beautiful face (often portrayed with woman's features) that once carried Muhammad.
Hantu Raya in Malay culture and spirituality (formerly a mix of animism, Hinduism, and Buddhism). The owner of a Hantu Raya is said to have formed a pact with a demon or inherited it from older generations as a legacy and in return for advantages and power, the owner agrees to provide for the ghost and appoints a new owner for it before dying. The creature resembles the look of its owner even after death and goes roaming.
